rationale_for_baseline_tier: A frail diabetic elder with a deep, infected, painless plantar ulcer, cellulitis spreading ~4cm proximally, malodorous purulence, and systemic upset (profound lethargy, flushing) on a background of severe hyperglycaemia (CBG 22.4 mmol/L) with missed insulin is a limb- AND life-threatening presentation. It carries a real, parallel risk of systemic sepsis and hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state, and requires same-day hands-on wound staging, bloods, imaging for osteomyelitis and often IV therapy — none obtainable via telehealth. T4 (ED, same-day, self/ambulance transport) is the correct tier: urgent in-person emergency assessment is needed now, escalating to T5/000 if sepsis or HHS features emerge. The painlessness and absence of fever are masking features and must not lower the tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The deep painless infected foot ulcer itself (baseline) — needs same-day in-person wound staging, offloading and workup
escalation_edges.rationale: A deep infected diabetic foot cannot be staged, probed for bone, imaged or cultured remotely; delay risks progression to osteomyelitis and amputation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This sore needs to be looked at in person today — it can't be safely assessed over the phone. Please arrange for him to be seen urgently at the emergency department, and keep all weight off that foot until then.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading rapidly up the foot/leg past the ankle, or increasing swelling and discharge
escalation_edges.rationale: Rapidly ascending cellulitis suggests invasive/necrotising soft-tissue infection or bacteraemia — an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading quickly up the leg past the ankle, call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New confusion, increasing drowsiness, or vomiting (hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state / severe metabolic crisis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Confusion/drowsiness/vomiting with CBG 22.4 signals impending HHS — a life-threatening metabolic emergency requiring ambulance transport.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes confused, very drowsy or starts vomiting, call 000 immediately — this can be a dangerous diabetic emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with uncontrollable shaking/rigors, or he becomes very unwell (possible sepsis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with rigors plus a deep infective source indicates systemic sepsis needing emergency care; frail diabetics may deteriorate rapidly.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he spikes a fever with shaking or becomes very unwell, call 000 — this could be a serious blood infection.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail older adult with dense neuropathy who under-reports and does not feel the wound; frail elders and diabetics often do not mount fever
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not down-tier on the absence of pain or fever — treat an acute change in a frail diabetic as an emergency until reversible/serious causes are excluded.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Relies on a visiting community caregiver; caregiver is the reporting party and will be enacting the safety-net
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct clear, concrete instructions to the caregiver (non-weight-bearing, red flags, how to get same-day assessment) and confirm the caregiver can arrange urgent transport.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Poorly controlled diabetes causes relative immunosuppression and impaired wound healing; likely nephropathy affects drug dosing
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises urgency and the threshold for hospital assessment; antibiotic and insulin decisions must be in-person with renal function known.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This needs urgent in-person emergency assessment today — please take him to the emergency department now (or call an ambulance if transport is a problem or he worsens). Keep all weight off the right foot completely (wheelchair, or crawl) until he is seen. Take his medicines and blood glucose meter with you.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day Emergency Department attendance; call 000 for an ambulance if he deteriorates or transport is not available
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: The ED and 000 are available 24 hours — this must not wait for clinic hours or the next in-hours GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the ED with the diabetes history, missed evening insulin due to poor vision, home CBG 22.4, the wound description and worsening cellulitis; request wound swab, bloods (glucose/ketones/osmolality, FBE/CRP/U&E/eGFR, cultures), foot imaging and High-Risk Foot Service referral. GP to review within 24-48h if community-managed after specialist assessment.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the painless foot and absence of fever as reassurance and routing to routine/next-day GP or self-care, missing a limb- and life-threatening infected diabetic foot with HHS/sepsis risk.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ascending infection progresses to sepsis or osteomyelitis requiring amputation; untreated HHS causes obtundation and death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Same-day urgent in-person care is the floor, but a plain urgent-care route without ED-level bloods/imaging may under-manage the systemic hyperglycaemia and sepsis risk, delaying recognition of HHS/sepsis/osteomyelitis.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an emergency ambulance for a patient who is currently haemodynamically stable and could self-transport to ED same-day (unless deteriorating) — low harm (ambulance resource use/alarm) and clearly preferable to under-triage, which is weighted 3x.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the patient already has systemic sepsis or established HHS, and whether the deep wound involves bone (osteomyelitis) — none determinable via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Assume the worse plausible pathway in a frail diabetic and arrange urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment with bloods and imaging; safety-net explicitly to 000 for sepsis/HHS features.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Reassure and defer to a routine GP review, or attempt remote antibiotic/insulin management, because the foot is painless and there is no reported fever.
